coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots/TiO2 enhanced visible light-assisted photocatalytic of leachate: Simultaneous effects and Mechanism insights
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qian
Wang, Tong
Laila, Noureen
Huang, Ke
Wang, Xinwei
Lei, Renbo
Bai, Xinyue
Xu, Qiyong
coredata.dc:description: The persistence and potential fouling risks associated with humic substances and bacteria present in leachate have gained increasing attention. Therefore, developing efficient and environmentally compatible technologies for their removal is essential. This study presented the hydrothermal synthesis of a photocatalyst by coupling carbon dots (CDs) and bulk TiO2 (P25). The incorporation of CDs increased the photocatalytic performance by enhancing visible light absorption and facilitating the separation of electrons/holes. Compared to P25, the CDs/P25 exhibited optimal photocatalytic activity for humic acid (HA), fulvic acid (FA), and leachate, with 1.64, 1.02, and 1.12 times higher activity, respectively. Remarkedly, the CDs/P25 accelerated the conversion of large HA molecules into small molecules at a faster rate and higher amount than the bulk P25, due to the increase of hydroxyl radicals, monoclinic oxygen radicals, and superoxide radicals. Additionally, the CDs/P25 demonstrated better bacterial-deactivation ability than the P25, with dead bacteria percentages of 83.3% and 34.6%, respectively. This study provides a promising strategy for efficiently applying CDs/P25 photocatalysis to leachate treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Leachate
Humic substances
Photocatalytic
Bacteria

Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22176005 ), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation ( 2021A1515110724 ), and Shenzhen Science and Technology Program ( RCBS20221008093224018 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic for preparing CDs/P25 catalyst; b) TEM images of CDs/P25 and HRTEM images; b-1) anatase TiO2 plane; b-2) rutile TiO2 plane; b-3) graphitic carbon plane; c) EDS elemental mapping images of Ti; d) EDS elemental mapping images of O; e) EDS elemental mapping images of C; f) EDS elemental mapping images of N.
a) XRD results of CDs/P25 and P25; b) FTIR total spectra and enlarged image of CDs/P25 and P25; c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms; XPS curve-fitting of d) Ti 2p; e) O 1s; f) C 1s.

a) PL spectra of the CDs/P25 and P25; b) UV-vis absorption spectrum of the CDs/P25 and P25; c) valence band (VB) XPS spectra of the CDs/P25 and P25; d) energy band structures of the CDs/P25 and P25; e) EIS Nyquist plot of the CDs/P25 and P25 at 0.5 V; f) EIS Nyquist plot of the CDs/P25 and P25 at 1.1 V.

a) photocatalytic degradation kinetics of UV254 in different solutions; b) degradation rate constants of UV254 in different solutions; c) photocatalytic degradation kinetics of VIS400 in different solutions; d) degradation rate constants of VIS400 in different solutions.

EEM spectra upon initial concentration of a) HA, b) FA, and c) leachate; degradation by the CDs/P25 with 240 min reaction of d) HA, e) FA, and f) leachate; composition distribution in different catalyst of g) HA, h) FA, and i) leachate.
EPR test results for a) •OH, b) 1O2, and c) •O2−in the CDs/P25 at 60 min photocatalytic time. EPR test results for d) •OH, e) 1O2, and f) •O2 −in the P25 at 60 min photocatalytic time.

a) Possible degradation pathways of humic acid in the CDs/P25 and P25 under visible light irradiation; b) LC-MS chromatograms of monoethyl phosphate in the CDs/P25 and P25; c) LC-MS chromatograms of adipic acid in the CDs/P25 and P25.

CLSM image of bacteria in the a) leachate; CLSM images of bacteria in the leachate after photocatalytic with the addition of b) P25, and c) CDs/P25; d) Q-PCR results of bacteria quantities; e) distribution of live and dead cells; f) microbial community at the genus level.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots/TiO2 enhanced visible light-assisted photocatalytic of leachate: Simultaneous effects and Mechanism insights

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Energy, Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School, University Town, Xili, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518055, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: †School of Environment and Energy, Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School, University Town, Xili, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518055, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Advanced Materials, Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School, University Town, Xili, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518055, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: ⊥School of Advanced Materials, Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School, University Town, Xili, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518055, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The persistence and potential fouling risks associated with humic substances and bacteria present in leachate have gained increasing attention. Therefore, developing efficient and environmentally compatible technologies for their removal is essential. This study presented the hydrothermal synthesis of a photocatalyst by coupling carbon dots (CDs) and bulk TiO2 (P25). The incorporation of CDs increased the photocatalytic performance by enhancing visible light absorption and facilitating the separation of electrons/holes. Compared to P25, the CDs/P25 exhibited optimal photocatalytic activity for humic acid (HA), fulvic acid (FA), and leachate, with 1.64, 1.02, and 1.12 times higher activity, respectively. Remarkedly, the CDs/P25 accelerated the conversion of large HA molecules into small molecules at a faster rate and higher amount than the bulk P25, due to the increase of hydroxyl radicals, monoclinic oxygen radicals, and superoxide radicals. Additionally, the CDs/P25 demonstrated better bacterial-deactivation ability than the P25, with dead bacteria percentages of 83.3% and 34.6%, respectively. This study provides a promising strategy for efficiently applying CDs/P25 photocatalysis to leachate treatment.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Leachate treatment is a significant challenge in the management of municipal solid waste (MSW) (Xiao et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Leachate is inevitably produced during different MSW treatment processes, such as landfill, incineration, and anaerobic digesters (Vyas et al., 2022). It was reported that billions of leachate are generated annually worldwide (Ma et al., 2022). Leachate is a complex wastewater containing large amounts of organic matter, various bacteria, and inorganic salts (Luo et al., 2020b; Teng et al., 2021). Without appropriate treatment, leachate can cause detrimental impacts on the surrounding environment and human health (Ye et al., 2019). Therefore, it is critical to develop efficient and cost-effective leachate treatment technologies.

The presence of humic substances (HS) in leachate, accounting for over 50% (wt%) of typical non-biodegradable organic matter, poses significant challenges for efficient leachate treatment (Kang et al., 2002). The structure and types of HS are complex, primarily including humic acid (HA) and fulvic acid (FA) (Xiao et al., 2022). HS contains abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups with aromatic structures, making them difficult to remove and limiting leachate treatment efficiency (Xue et al., 2020). Additionally, abundant bacteria and their related activities in leachate lead to the formation of biofilms, consequently posing fouling and clogging during treatment (Meng et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2022a). Thus, simultaneous removal of HS and bacteria is urgently needed to improve leachate treatment efficiency.

Photocatalytic degradation has attracted worldwide attention for leachate treatment, owing to its low cost, environmental friendliness, non-toxic, and chemically stable (Noureen et al., 2022; Pervez et al., 2020). So far, titanium dioxide (TiO2)-P25 is a widely used commercial semiconductor photocatalyst, including anatase and rutile crystallizes (Hassan et al., 2016). A recent study showed that TiO2 could reduce the humification in HA and transform it into small molecules (Zhu et al., 2018). In addition, TiO2 can enhance the antibacterial performance of membrane bioreactors (Vatanpour et al., 2022). However, the wide band gap of TiO2 limits its utilization of only UV light (<5% of the total solar spectrum), leading to low photodegradation efficiency due to high electron-hole recombination rates (Song et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2020). To overcome these limitations, lots of effects are focused on developing TiO2 technology capable of exploiting visible light to improve photocatalysis efficiency.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) has been investigated as a promising co-photocatalyst due to their unique properties, including good electron transport, light absorption, low toxicity, and biocompatibility (Yu et al., 2023). Previous research has investigated the coupling of CDs with semiconductors to extend sunlight absorption and enhance photocatalytic performance for organic pollutant degradation. For instance, Ke et al. (2017) demonstrated that coupling CDs and TiO2 could increase the utilization efficiency of visible light for the photodegradation of organic dyes (methylene blue). Zhao et al. (2022) reported that CDs modified P25 exhibited high activation and conversion efficiency of glucose. However, to the best of the authors' knowledge, the anchoring of CDs with TiO2 to improve the photocatalytic ability of HS and antibacterial properties in leachate has not been explored.

Previous studies reported the application of TiO2 photocatalytic degradation for HA and bacteria separately in leachate (Zhu et al., 2018; Vatanpour et al., 2022). However, there is still a need to explore the utilization of visible light in photocatalytic technology development (Teng et al., 2021). In this study, we aimed to fabricate CDs coupled with P25 photocatalytic material to enhance sunlight absorption and extend the photocatalytic activity. This study aims to investigate the photocatalytic efficiency and mechanisms of CDs coupled with P25 for the degradation of HS and bacteria in leachate. The fabricated catalyst would be applied to degrade of HA and FA in leachate. Then, the contribution of radicals and degradation pathways was investigated to comprehensively understand the photocatalytic process. Moreover, the antibacterial performance of the catalyst was evaluated in leachate, including microbial community, quantitative PCR (Q-PCR), and bacterial status. The findings of this study provide new insights into the degradation behavior of HS and the antibacterial performance of the CDs/P25, thus offering a feasible approach for leachate treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots- TiO2 P25
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots- TiO2 P25 (CDs/P25) photocatalyst was synthesized based on a previous study (Li et al., 2018). First, citric acid monohydrate (CA, 2.52 g) was dissolved in 60 mL ultrapure water with magnetic stirring, adjusting pH to 6 using NH3·OH. Then, TiO2-P25 (0.96 g) was added to the solution, and the mixture was subjected to magnetic stirring and ultrasonic for 30 min. The mixture was added to a hydrothermal Teflon autoclave and heated at 160°C for 4 h. Finally, the sediments were collected through centrifugation, washed with ethanol and ultrapure water and dried at 60°C for 12 h. The materials and reagents used in the synthesis are provided in the Supplementary.

The morphology and size of the synthesized photocatalyst were obtained by transmission electron microscopy (Thermo Scientific, Talos F200S G2 S). The crystal lattice and crystalline surface were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (Rigaku, Dmax 2500V) under Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 150 mA) with a scan range from 5° to 90°. The element composition was determined by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Shimadzu, IRTracer-100) scanning from 4000-400 cm−1. The adsorption-desorption isotherms of N2 were obtained by an automatic specific surface area analyzer (Micromeritics, ASAP 2020 PLUS HD88). The element composition of the photocatalyst was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher, Escalab 250Xi).

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of photocatalysts were detected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, LS 55). The UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of photocatalysts were performed on UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, lambda 1050) by using BaSO4 as a reference. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were obtained by an electrochemical workstation (Zahner, Zennium C-IMPS) in a three-electrode system with Pt- Ag/AgCl working electrode.

The photocatalytic experiments were performed using a self-made reactor equipped with a Xenon lamp (300 W, ceaulight, CEL-HXF) to stimulate sunlight irradiation (Fig. S1). According to previous studies (Martins et al., 2016, Sui et al., 2019), the distance between the light source and the reaction solution ranged from 10 cm to 15 cm. Moreover, the light intensity was set at 0.2 W/cm2 based on a previous study (Chen et al., 2012). To achieve 0.2 W/cm2 in this study, we tested the light intensity between 10 and 15 cm distance using an irradiator (Beijing Normal University Photoelectric Instrument Factory, FZ-A). Thus, we set the distance between the light source and the reaction solution at 12 cm with a light intensity of the reactant center of 0.2 ± 0.0002 W/cm2. The reaction temperature was kept at 25±2°C. The initial pH values of different solutions are illustrated in Table S1. The initial pH of the CDs/P25 in the HA solution, FA solution, and leachate were 12.47±0.03, 6.57±0.01, and 8.48±0.01, respectively. The initial pH of the P25 in the HA solution, FA solution, and leachate were 12.42±0.01, 6.18±0.12, and 8.33±0.06, respectively. First, 0.5 g/L of photocatalyst was added into 150 mL reaction solution containing 50 mg/L HA, 50 mg/L FA, and leachate. To achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium of photocatalyst, the reaction solution was stirred in the dark for 60 min, as described in a previous reference (Xu et al., 2023). Second, the photocatalytic experiment was stirred under simulated sunlight for 240 min at the stirring speed of 400 r/min. Samples were collected at 30 min intervals and centrifuged for 15 min at 12000 rpm, followed by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter membrane.

The degradation rates of HA and FA were determined by measuring the ultraviolet and visible light absorbance at 254 nm and 400 nm, respectively. The EEM spectra of different samples were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, F-7000) by scanning the emission wavelengths (Em) from 280 to 560 nm and the excitation wavelengths (Ex) from 220 to 420 at wavelength increments of 5 nm and 1 nm, respectively. The humification index (HIX) was calculated based on the EEM spectra under the Em from 430-480 nm to 300-345 nm at Ex of 255 nm (Maqbool et al., 2020). The dissolved organic carbon of the solutions was measured by a TOC analyzer (Shimadzu, TOC-L CSN). The formation of active radicals was analyzed using the electron paramagnetic resonance method (Bruker, A300). The components of solutions were detected by LC-MS/MS (Thermo Scientific, Q Exactive Focus).

The antibacterial activity of the CDs/P25 photocatalyst was evaluated using leachate collected from a Shenzhen landfill. Prior to use, all materials and glassware were sterilized in an autoclave (GI54, Zealway, USA) at 120°C for 30 min. Antibacterial tests were conducted using the same photocatalytic procedures as described in Section 2.4. After the reaction, the aliquots were collected for the 16 S rRNA sequencing analysis (Miseq, Illumina, USA), quantitative PCR (ABI7300, Applied Biosystems, USA), and bacterial cell viability. For 16 S rRNA sequencing analysis and quantitative PCR were 338F (5′-ACTCCTACGGGAGGCAGCAG-3′)/806R (5′-GGACTACHVGGGTWTCTAAT-3′) bacterial primer set. The bacterial cell viability was performed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (A1 R, Nikon, Japan) with two dyes, including SYTO9 and PI for live and dead bacteria, respectively.

The energy consumption and cost analysis are critical for the practical application of CDs/P25. For energy efficiency, the energy consumption for TOC removal was determined as the following equation:
E C =
P T
(
T O
C T
− T O
C O
)
V
Where
E C
represents energy consumption (kWh/gTOC), P is the power of Xenon lamp (kW), T is the reaction time (h),
T O
C T
and
T O
C O
are the TOC concentration (mg/L) at a given time and original time, respectively, V is the reaction volume (L).

In terms of the cost, the materials cost of CDs/P25 were calculated, including citric acid monohydrate (CA), ultrapure water, P25, and NH3·OH. The cost for CDs/P25 synthesis was determined as the following equation:
C =
m
C A
p
C A
+
m w
p w
+
m p
p p
+
m n
p n
M
Where C represents the cost of the CDs/P25 (dollars/g),
m
C A
,
m w
,
m p
, and
m n
are the amount (g) of CA, ultrapure water, P25, and NH3·OH, respectively.
p
C A
,
p w
,
p p
, and
p n
are the price (dollars/g) of CA, ultrapure water, P25, and NH3·OH, respectively. M is the production amount of CDs/P25 (g). The price and source of data used for cost analysis were summarized in Table S2.

The composition and morphology of the CDs/P25 catalyst significantly affect its photocatalytic performance. The CDs/P25 catalysts were synthesized following a hydrothermal method with slight modification (Fig. 1 a). The morphology and composition of the CDs/P25 catalyst were characterized using TEM measurements. The TEM images revealed that the CDs/P25 catalysts exhibited a sphere-like structure with side lengths ranging from 18.8-22.2 nm, with the CDs deposited on the surface of P25 in a dispersed manner. Similar structure and size were observed in the TEM images of P25 (Fig. S1), indicating that the modification of CDs does not affect the size of P25. Three lattice fringes were observed on the surface of CDs/P25. The interplanar distances of 0.237 nm and 0.248 nm corresponded to the anatase TiO2 (004) plane (JCPDS card no. 99-0008) and rutile TiO2 (101) plane (JCPDS card no. 21-1276), respectively (Zhou et al., 2021). The lattice spacing of CDs in the figure is 0.338 nm, attributed to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon (Zhao et al., 2019). The elemental mapping of CDs/P25 is illustrated in Fig. 1c-1f, including four elements of Ti, O, C, and N. The C element is evenly distributed in CDs/P25, indicating that the CDs were uniformly modified on the surface of CDs/P25. The presence of N was attributed to the addition of aqueous ammonia during the preparation process of CDs/P25.

The crystal structures of CDs/P25 and P25 were compared in Fig. 2 a. In the CDs/P25 and P25, the strong peak at 2θ of 25.303°, 36.948°, 37.792°, 48.035° and 75.043° correspond to the (101), (103), (004), (200), and (215) reflection peaks of the anatase TiO2, respectively. Similarly, the existence peaks at 2θ values of 54.322° and 56.640° are the characteristic diffraction peaks of the rutile TiO2. However, no distinct diffraction peak related to graphitic carbon was observed in the CDs/P25, indicating the well dispersion and low crystallinity of doped CDs (Saud et al., 2015; Sui et al., 2019). As illustrated in Fig. 2 b, the FTIR spectra were performed to compare the functional groups and bonding compositions of the catalysts. In the cases of CDs/P25 and P25, the band at 3440 and 1630 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching and bending vibration of the surface hydroxyl (O-H) group, respectively (Mukthar Ali et al., 2019). However, new bands at 2920, 1450, and 1400 cm−1 were observed in the CDs/P25, which were attributed to the stretching vibration of C-H, C-N=, and C-O-C, respectively (Dar et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2020a). These new bands suggested the successful coupling of CDs and P25. The pore structure and surface area of the CDs/P25 and P25 were detected by the N2 adsorption-desorption method (Fig. 2c). Both CDs/P25 and P25 showed type Ⅲ isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, suggesting that the pores were mainly flat slit shapes. In addition, the CDs/P25 exhibited a larger pore size than the P25, indicating that the CDs/P25 was conducive to the diffusion of active radicals and improved the photocatalytic degradation ability (Gao et al., 2023). Two peaks centered at 459.1 and 464.8 eV in the Ti 2p spectrum were associated with Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 binding energy, respectively (Fig. 2d). For the O1s peaks of CDs/P25 and P25, the typical peaks at 530.4 eV, 531.7 eV, and 532.7 eV were attributed to the bonds of Ti-O, C-O, and O-H, respectively (Sui et al., 2019). In addition, it was found that the CDs/P25 contained C-C (285.1 eV), C-O (286.1 eV), and C=O (286.7 eV). The CDs/P25 contained more C elements than P25, owing to the combination of CDs (Fig. 2f). These results suggested the successful loading of CDs and TiO2 on the surface of the CDs/P25.

The CDs/P25 showed lower emission intensity compared to P25 (Fig. 3 a). The lower PL intensity indicates a high electron/hole separation rate, suggesting a higher efficiency of photoreactions (Saud et al., 2015). As illustrated in Fig. 3b, P25 exhibited almost no absorption in the visible light region, whereas the CDs/P25 showed an increasing absorption ability in the visible light region, in accordance with the color of the catalyst changing from white to yellow. The VB edge of P25 was 2.68 eV, while the CDs/P25 showed a red shift to 2.62 eV. The band gap in the CDs/P25 (3.24 eV) was narrower than that in the P25 (3.26 eV), indicating that the photoinduced electrons (e−) were more easily transferred from the VB to the conduction band (CB) in the CDs/P25 (Qin et al., 2020). The photoinduced migration efficiency and charge separation of the CDs/P25 and P25 were compared (Fig.3 e f). The arc radius of the CDs/P25 Nyquist was smaller than that of the P25, indicating the presence of CDs improved the exciton dissociation and charge transfer property, making it easier to contain more electron-holes in reactions (Xu et al., 2023).

As shown in Fig. 4 a), the CDs/P25 showed a higher UV254 degradation ability than the P25 in the HA, FA, and leachate. For the P25, only approximately 45%, 97%, and 77% of HA, FA, and leachate were degraded after 240 min irradiation, respectively. In contrast, remarkable improvements were achieved in the HA, FA, and leachate with the combination of CDs. The photocatalytic efficiency of the CDs/P25 was 74%, 99%, and 86% in the HA, FA, and leachate, respectively. The removal efficiency constants (k) in the CDs/P25 were 2.0 times higher than these of the P25 (Fig 4b). Moreover, in the case of VIS400, the CDs/P25 showed higher elimination efficiency than the P25 in the HA, FA, and leachate. The kinetic degradation rate constant of the CDs/P25 (0.35 min−1) was 1.7 times higher than that of the P25 (0.21 min−1). The UV254 is regarded as an indicator of aromatic and refractory substances (Altmann et al., 2016), while the VIS400 is used to signify color-forming moieties (Katsumata et al., 2008). In addition, the CDs/P25 showed a higher TOC degradation ability than the P25 in the HA, FA, and leachate (Fig.S3 a). For the P25, only 90%, 88%, and 39% of TOC in the HA, FA, and leachate were removed after 240 min photocatalytic, respectively. Conversely, the removal efficiency of the CDs/P25 was 96%, 97%, and 49% in the HA, FA, and leachate, respectively. The kinetic degradation rate constants of the CDs/P25 were 1.3-1.6 times higher than these of the P25 (Fig. S3 b). Overall, these results indicated that combining CDs could enhance the removal of organic contents, especially aromatic substances and color-forming moieties in the humic substances.

As illustrated in Fig.5 a), the initial untreated HA showed one maximum at Ex/Em of 250-450 nm/380-560 nm (region Ⅴ) attributed to humic acids, while one maximum at Ex/Em of 220-250 nm/330-380 nm (region Ⅳ) was observed in the initial untreated FA. The leachate was rich in the regions Ⅱ, Ⅲ, Ⅳ, and Ⅴ, indicating a high pollutant concentration. After photocatalytic treatment, the multiple fluorescence peaks decreased. In the case of HA, the region Ⅴ in the P25 decreased from 37.0% (initial) to 21.4% (240 min), while the region Ⅴ in the CDs/P25 declined from 36.4% (initial) to 15.2% (240 min). Conversely, the percentage of regions Ⅰ-Ⅲ showed a great increasing trend in the CDs/P25 (21.13%) compared to the P25 (16.49%). A similar trend was observed in the FA with an increasing composition in the regions Ⅰ-Ⅲ. It suggested that photocatalytic treatment could decompose some humic acid components with a relatively large molecular size into small molecules (Zhu et al., 2018). However, due to the complexity of the leachate, the CDs/P25 transferred from regions Ⅲ and Ⅴ to regions I and Ⅳ in the leachate.

In addition, the humification index (HIX) of the P25 and CDs/P25 were compared in Table S3. For the HA group, the HIX of the CDs/P25 was 3.89 after photodegradation, which was lower than that of the P25 (6.26). A similar trend was observed in the FA and leachate. According to Huguet et al. (2009), HIX<4 and HIX>10 represent weak and strong humification, respectively. A lower degree of humification indicates a shorter existence time in the environment (Shang et al., 2023). These results further confirmed the stronger photodegradation ability of the CDs/P25 photocatalyst.

Based on the intermediates generated during the photocatalytic process, the degradation pathways of HA were speculated and shown in Fig. 7 a. The complete structure of the main intermediates generated during the photocatalytic process of HA with the CDs/P25 and P25 are listed in Table S4. HA is a complex organic matter that contains phenolic hydroxyl, carboxyl, and ketone groups as the main composition. The LC-MS results showed that the products after 60 min dark reaction were mainly nonanoic acid, palmitic acid, and stearic acid in both the CDs/P25 and P25. Interestingly, the CDs/P25 could also convert the HA into adipic acid after 60 min dark reactions. However, no active radicals and nonradicals were detected in the dark reaction (Fig. 6). These results indicated that the CDs/P25 could stimulate the open-loop and bond-breaking reactions of HA in the dark reactions without active radicals. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the dark reaction of HA during photocatalysis.

After 120 minutes of photocatalytic reactions, the HA and intermediates were further degraded into small molecules, such as caprylic acid and nonanoic acid in both the CDs/P25 and P25. The presence of triethylene glycol, caprolactam, and norleucine in the CDs/P25 was caused by the higher intensity of active radicals and nonradicals. Finally, after a 240-minute photocatalytic reaction, the HA was degraded into monoethyl phosphate, diisopropylethylamine, betaine, and caprolactam. For the monoethyl phosphate, the P25 only generated 5.55E+8 after 240 minutes of photocatalytic reaction (Fig. 7 b). However, the CDs/P25 contained monoethyl phosphate from the beginning of the photocatalytic reaction, and the amount of the monoethyl phosphate in the CDs/P25 (6.16E+8) after 240 minutes was higher than that in the P25. A converse trend was observed that the adipic acid in the CDs/P25 (5.01E+8) after 240 minutes was lower than that in the P25 (9.85E+08). Overall, these results indicated that the photocatalytic degradation of HA was derived from active free radicals and nonradicals (Cai et al., 2022). The CDs/P25 could stimulate the release of electrons and degrade HA into small molecules at a faster rate and higher amounts.

For the degradation of FA, a faster and complete degradation was observed in the CDs/P25 compared to the P25. As illustrated in Table S5, the dark intermediate in both CDs/P25 and P25 were mainly composed of adipic acid, nonanoic acid, and stearic acid, while the photocatalytic intermediates were triaminopyrimidine, dihydroxybiphenyl, methyl pyrimidine, dihexyl phthalate. The amount of dihexyl phthalate in the CDs/P25 was 4.07E+5 after 240 min photocatalytic reaction, which was lower than that in the P25 (4.13E+6). In contrast, the amount of methyl pyrimidine in the CDs/P25 was lower than that in the P25 (Fig. S4). These results indicated that the CDs/P25 could facilitate the transformation of FA into small molecules.

The leachate contained more complex organic matter than the HA and FA. As shown in Table S6, the CDs/P25 degraded the humic substances in the leachate into larger amounts of some molecules. For example, the abundance of malonic acid in the CDs/P25 was 3.90E+7 at 240 min photocatalytic reactions, which was twice that in the P25 (Fig. S5). Above all, the CDs/P25 could degrade HA, FA, and leachate into small molecules with smaller molecular weights and higher content, owning to the faster electron-hole efficiency compared to the P25.

As illustrated in Fig. 8 a-c, green and red staining represented live and dead cells, respectively. The leachate was mainly composed of live bacteria (89.5%), while the P25 killed some bacteria, reducing live bacteria to 65.4%. Moreover, the CDs/P25 showed better antibacterial ability, with 83.3% dead bacteria (Fig. 8d). The Q-PCR results represent the quantitative analysis of bacteria in different samples (Fig. 8e). The bacterial gene copies in the leachate, P25, and CDs/P25 were 1.62E+9, 5.97E+8, and 1.98E+8 copies/mL, respectively. Above all, the CDs/P25 exhibited better bacterial-deactivation ability than the P25. In addition, the presence of P25 and CDs/P25 changed the microbial community structure in the leachate. Bacillus (21.2%) was the primary bacterial type in the leachate at the genus level, which was considered a typical bio-clogging bacteria in leachate (Wang et al., 2022b). Notably, the percentage of Bacillus in the P25 was 18.6%, while the CDs/P25 was composed of 12.8% Bacillus. In contrast, the percentage of Raineyella in the leachate, P25, and CDs/P25 was 13.4%,21.2%, and 25.4%, respectively. These results indicated that photocatalysts could change the microbial community, especially Bacillus, a sensitive bacteria type. Raineyella was identified as a novel bacterial resource for bio-clogging that has not been reported before. The coupling of CDs could kill more bacteria due to its higher photoinduced charge separation efficiency and cause oxidative damage (Yu et al., 2023).

The recovery rate of photocatalysts is critical for their widespread application. Therefore, the recovery mass percentages of CDs/P25 in different solutions were compared (Fig. S6). The recovery rates of CDs/P25 in the HA and leachate were 91% and 83%, respectively, indicating that the CDs/P25 were recyclable and stable. Moreover, the pH values were stable in both CDs/P25 and P25 after photocatalytic performance (Fig. S7). These results further indicated the stability of the CDs/P25. As illustrated in Fig. S8, the CDs/P25 led to an apparent decrease in energy consumption compared to P25. The energy consumption of CDs/P25 in the HA was 454.8 kWh/gTOC, which was 46.7% of the energy consumption of P25. The energy consumption of CDs/P25 and P25 in the FA were 340.9 and 520.8 kWh/gTOC, respectively. In addition, the energy consumption of CDs/P25 in the leachate was 689.1 kWh/gTOC, while 1142.9 kWh/gTOC in the P25. These results indicated that CDs/P25 could increase the degradation efficiency, thus reducing energy consumption. Moreover, the cost of the CDs/P25 was 0.4 dollars/g, while the commercial P25 ranged from 0.2 to 3.4 dollars/g. The price of the CDs/P25 was comparable to the commercial P25, which indicated that the CDs/P25 was economically feasible for practical application.

In addition, the technical feasibility of the CDs/P25 was evaluated through photodegradation in commercial HA, commercial FA, and practical leachate. As shown in Section 3.3, the reduced UV254 and Vis400 followed the order of FA>leachate>HA. The intermediate degradation level of leachate might be attributed to its complex composition, which contains organic matter, bacteria, and inorganic salts (Luo et al., 2020b). Likewise, the CDs/P25could remove other pollutants in leachate. For example, with the photocatalytic performance of CDs/P25, the alkalinity of the leachate decreased from 613.9 mg/L to 363.9 mg/L (Table S7). Overall, the excellent pollutant removal performance of the CDs/P25 in leachate indicated its potential as a technology for treating practical leachate.

In summary, the hydrothermal synthesized CDs/P25 exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance for the degradation of HA, FA, and leachate under simulated sunlight irradiation. Compared to bulk P25, the CDs/P25 showed optimal photocatalytic activity for the degradation of HA, FA, and leachate, which was 1.64, 1.02, and 1.12 times higher than bulk P25, respectively. Moreover, the CDs/P25 exhibited high efficiency in converting large molecules into small constituents, such as caprolactam, betaine, monoethyl phosphate, and diisopropylethylamine. It achieved the conversion in only one-fifth of the time required by the P25. Furthermore, the CDs/P25 showed better antibacterial ability than the P25, resulting in an increase in bacteria mortality from 34.6% to 83.3%. The CDs/P25 possessed easily recycled and favorable technical feasibility. These advantages were attributed to coupled CDs, which not only enhanced the absorption of visible light and lowered the band gap, but also promoted the separation of electron-hole pairs and generated more reactive radicals during photocatalytic. Overall, this study demonstrated that CDs/P25 photocatalysts combined the advantages of visible light utilization and electron-holes separation, demonstrating the excellent potential for leachate treatment applications containing HA, FA, and bacteria. For future research, it is recommended to further evaluate the degradation efficiency of different light intensities and initial pH values. This would help validate and expand the applications of CDs/P25 in leachate treatment.

Qian Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Tong Wang: Investigation, Visualization. Noureen Laila: Writing – review & editing. Ke Huang: Visualization. Xinwei Wang: Writing – review & editing. Renbo Lei: Investigation. Xinyue Bai: Validation. Qiyong Xu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176005), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2021A1515110724), and Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (RCBS20221008093224018).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.watres.2023.120659.
